content:
🚨 Researchers just proved that every major AI model fails at the same thing.
Not reasoning. Not coding. Not math.
Building tools from scratch.
Tool-Genesis gave AI agents a real-world task with zero pre-written specs. No schemas. No documentation. Just: figure out what tools you need and build them.
The results:
→ GPT-5.1: 37% success rate in single-pass mode
→ Gemini-3-Flash: 10%
→ Claude Haiku: effectively 0%
-The models could write compliant-looking schemas. They just couldn't build tools that actually worked.
-And the failures didn't happen at the end. They happened at the start.
A tiny interface error in step one cascades through the entire pipeline:
Wrong parameter name. Missed constraint. Ambiguous type.
By the final task, that small mistake has compounded into a complete failure.
The paper calls this the "utility-conversion bottleneck."
High compliance scores. Plausible-looking schemas. Zero downstream success.
One thing actually fixes it: execution feedback in a loop.
When models could run their own code, catch errors, and repair them:
→ Gemini jumped from 10% to 58%
→ Qwen3-235B went from 29% to 47%
The model that looks weakest in a single pass often recovers the most with a repair loop.
The next capability frontier isn't bigger context windows.
It's closed-loop tool creation from abstract requirements.
